en.extract:
The pink-backed pelican (Pelecanus rufescens) is a bird of the pelican family. It is a resident breeder in the swamps and shallow lakes of Africa and southern Arabia; it has also been extirpated from Madagascar.


Taxonomy
The pink-backed pelican was formally described in 1789 by the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin in his revised and expanded edition of Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae. He placed it with the other pelicans in the genus Pelecanus and coined the binomial name Pelecanus rufescens. Gmelin based his description on the "red-backed pelican" that had been described in 1785 by the English ornithologist John Latham in his multi-volume work A General Synopsis of Birds. Latham had been sent a specimen from West Africa. The specific epithet rufescens is Latin for "reddish". The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description

It is a relatively small pelican, although by no means is it a small bird. Its length is from 125 to 155 cm (49 to 61 in), wingspan is 2.15–2.9 m (7.1–9.5 ft) and body mass is from 4 to 7 kg (8.8 to 15.4 lb). The bill is 30 to 38 cm (12 to 15 in) in length. The plumage is grey and white, with a pinkish hue on the back occasionally apparent (never in the deep pink of a flamingo). The top of the bill is yellow and the pouch is usually greyish. Breeding adults have long feather plumes on the head.
It shares its habitat with the great white pelican, which is generally larger and has white instead of greyish plumage.


Habitat and breeding
The pink-backed pelican is found in a range of aquatic habitats, but prefers quiet backwaters with shallow water, avoiding steep, vegetated lake banks. It prefers for freshwater lakes, swamps, large slow-flowing rivers, and seasonal pools but also frequents reservoirs, seasonally flooded land and flood-plains near river mouths. It may occur on alkaline and saline lakes and lagoons, and can sometimes be found along the coast in bays and estuaries (although seldom on open seashore). The species tends to roost and breed in trees (e.g. mangroves), but will also roost on sandy islands, cliffs, coral reefs and sand-dunes.
Nesting trees have many nests built close together. These nests are re-used every year until the trees collapse, although the birds will normally remain in the area. The species nests colonially in trees, reeds or low bushes along waterfronts as well as (less often) on the ground on sandy islands and in mangroves.
Its nest is a large heap of sticks and may be 10–50 m (33–164 ft) above the ground. The female lays two to three large white eggs and later the chicks feed by plunging their heads deep into the adult's pouch and taking the partially digested regurgitated fish.


Diet
Food is usually fish (of any size up to 450 g (16 oz), usually in the 80–290 g (2.8–10.2 oz) range) and amphibians, and is usually obtained by fishing in groups. Among the fish preyed upon are cichlids like Haplochromis and Tilapia.


References


External links
Explore Species: Pink-backed Pelican at eBird (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Pinkbacked Pelican in The Atlas of Southern African Birds

The '''pink-backed pelican''' ('''''Pelecanus rufescens''''') is a [[bird]] of the [[pelican]] family. It is a resident breeder in the [[swamp]]s and shallow lakes of [[Africa]] and southern [[Arabian Peninsula|Arabia]]; it has also been [[Local extinction|extirpate]]d from [[Madagascar]].

==Taxonomy==
The pink-backed pelican was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1789 by the German naturalist [[Johann Friedrich Gmelin]] in his revised and expanded edition of [[Carl Linnaeus]]'s ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the other pelicans in the [[genus]] ''[[Pelecanus]]'' and coined the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Pelecanus rufescens''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gmelin | first=Johann Friedrich | author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin| year=1789 | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=13th | volume=1, Part 2 | language=Latin | location=Lipsiae [Leipzig] | publisher=Georg. Emanuel. Beer | page=571 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2656064 }}</ref> Gmelin based his description on the "red-backed pelican" that had been described in 1785 by the English ornithologist [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] in his multi-volume work ''A General Synopsis of Birds''. Latham had been sent a specimen from West Africa.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Latham | first=John | author-link=John Latham (ornithologist) | year=1785 | title=A General Synopsis of Birds | volume=3, Part 2 | publisher=Printed for Leigh and Sotheby | location=London | page=584, No. 6 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40079407 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=1 | edition=2nd | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=190 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16108830 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''rufescens'' is [[Latin]] for "reddish".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=341 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n341/mode/1up }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=August 2022 | title=Ibis, spoonbills, herons, Hamerkop, Shoebill, pelicans | work=IOC World Bird List Version 12.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pelicans/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=2 December 2022 }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Pink backed pelican dorsal.jpg|thumb|left|Dorsal view showing characteristic pink back]]
[[File:Pelecanus rufescens MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.12.3.jpg|thumb| ''Pelecanus rufescens'' – [[MHNT]]]]
[[File:Pelecanus rufescens - Étang de Bages-Sigean 01.jpg|thumb|left|400 px|Formation flying]]
It is a relatively small pelican, although by no means is it a small bird. Its length is from {{convert|125|to|155|cm|in|abbr=on}}, wingspan is {{convert|2.15|-|2.9|m|ft|abbr=on}} and body mass is from {{convert|4|to|7|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. The bill is {{convert|30|to|38|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-pink-backed-pelican.html |year=2011 |title=Pink-backed Pelican {{!}} Pelecanus rufescens |website=Oiseaux-birds}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Pink-backed Pelicans {{!}} Pelecanus rufescens |website=Israeli Bird Stamps |url=http://israelibirdsstamps.yardbirds.org.il/g2.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120425064858/http://israelibirdsstamps.yardbirds.org.il/g2.html |archive-date=25 April 2012 |year=2011}}</ref> The plumage is grey and white, with a pinkish hue on the back occasionally apparent (never in the deep pink of a [[flamingo]]). The top of the bill is yellow and the pouch is usually greyish. Breeding adults have long [[feather]] plumes on the head.

It shares its habitat with the [[great white pelican]], which is generally larger and has white instead of greyish plumage.

==Habitat and breeding==
The pink-backed pelican is found in a range of aquatic habitats, but prefers quiet backwaters with shallow water, avoiding steep, vegetated lake banks. It prefers for freshwater lakes, [[swamp]]s, large slow-flowing rivers, and seasonal pools but also frequents reservoirs, seasonally flooded land and flood-plains near river mouths. It may occur on alkaline and saline lakes and lagoons, and can sometimes be found along the coast in bays and [[estuary|estuaries]] (although seldom on open seashore). The species tends to roost and breed in trees (e.g. mangroves), but will also roost on sandy islands, cliffs, [[coral reef]]s and sand-dunes.

Nesting trees have many [[bird nest|nests]] built close together. These nests are re-used every year until the trees collapse, although the birds will normally remain in the area. The species nests colonially in trees, reeds or low bushes along waterfronts as well as (less often) on the ground on sandy islands and in [[mangrove]]s.

Its nest is a large heap of sticks and may be {{convert|10-50|m|||abbr=on}} above the ground. The female lays two to three large white [[bird egg|eggs]] and later the chicks feed by plunging their heads deep into the adult's pouch and taking the partially digested regurgitated fish.

==Diet==
Food is usually fish (of any size up to {{convert|450|g|||abbr=on}}, usually in the {{convert|80-290|g|||abbr=on}} range) and [[amphibia]]ns, and is usually obtained by fishing in groups. Among the fish preyed upon are [[cichlid]]s like ''[[Haplochromis]]'' and ''[[Tilapia (genus)|Tilapia]]''.

zh.extract:
粉背鵜鶘（學名：Pelecanus rufescens）是鵜鶘科的一種鳥類。牠們是非洲和阿拉伯半島南部沼澤與淺水湖泊的留鳥繁殖種；在馬達加斯加則已絕跡。


分類
粉背鵜鶘於1789年由德國博物學家約翰·弗里德里希·格梅林在其修訂擴充的卡爾·林奈自然系統一書中正式描述。他將牠與其他鵜鶘一同歸入鵜鶘屬（Pelecanus），並創立了二名法名稱 Pelecanus rufescens。 格梅林的描述基於1785年英國鳥類學家約翰·萊瑟姆在其多卷本著作《鳥類綜述》（A General Synopsis of Birds）中描述的「紅背鵜鶘」。萊瑟姆收到了來自西非的標本。 其種小名 rufescens 在拉丁語中意為「微紅色的」。 該物種是單型種：沒有公認的亞種。


描述

這種鵜鶘體型相對較小，但絕非小鳥。牠們的體長約125至155公分（49至61英寸），翼展2.15至2.9公尺（7.1至9.5英尺），體重4至7公斤（8.8至15.4磅）。喙長約30至38公分（12至15英寸）。 羽毛呈灰色和白色，背部偶爾略帶粉紅色調（從不會呈現如火烈鳥般的深粉紅色）。上喙呈黃色，喉囊通常為灰色。繁殖期的成鳥頭部有長長的羽飾。
牠與大白鵜鶘共享棲息地，大白鵜鶘體型通常更大，羽毛是純白色而非灰白色。


棲息地與繁殖
粉背鵜鶘棲息於多種水域環境，但偏好水流平緩、水淺的僻靜水域，避開陡峭、植被茂密的湖岸。牠們更喜歡淡水湖泊、沼澤、流速緩慢的大河以及季節性水塘，但也常在人工水庫、季節性氾濫區域和河口附近的氾濫平原活動。有時也會出現在鹼性湖、鹹水湖和潟湖，並偶爾在沿海的海灣和河口出沒（但很少在開闊的海岸邊）。此物種傾向於在樹上（如紅樹林）棲息和繁殖，但也會在沙島、懸崖、珊瑚礁和沙丘上棲息。
築巢的樹上常有許多巢緊密地建在一起。這些巢穴會每年重複使用，直到樹木倒塌為止，儘管鳥群通常仍會留在該區域。此物種在水邊的樹木、蘆葦叢或低矮灌木上群體築巢繁殖，較少在沙島或紅樹林的地面上築巢。
牠們的巢是由樹枝堆積而成的大巢，可能位於離地10至50公尺（33至164英尺）高的地方。雌鳥會產下兩到三顆大型白色的蛋。之後，雛鳥會將頭深深探入成鳥的喉囊中，取食成鳥反芻出來的半消化魚類。


食性
食物通常是魚類（大小可達450克（0.99磅），通常在80至290克（0.18至0.64磅）之間）和兩棲動物，通常通過群體協作捕魚獲得。牠們捕食的魚類包括慈鯛，如朴麗魚屬（Haplochromis）和羅非魚屬（Tilapia）的魚種。


參考文獻


外部連結
粉背鵜鶘（Pinkbacked Pelican）於《南部非洲鳥類地圖集》（The Atlas of Southern African Birds）
